items.q: What is the currently accepted age of the universe?
items.misconception: About 14 billion years — close enough for any purpose.
items.random_wrong: About 6 billion years, the same as the Earth.
items.why: Students round aggressively and don't appreciate that the precision (13.8, not 14) reflects measurements from the cosmic microwave background.

items.q: What are typical units for the Hubble constant?
items.correct: km per second per megaparsec (km/s/Mpc) — recession speed per unit distance.
items.misconception: Metres per second — it's the speed at which the universe expands.
items.random_wrong: Light-years — it's a measure of cosmic distance.
items.why: Students treat H0 as a speed or distance rather than a rate of expansion (speed per unit distance), missing the structure of Hubble's law.

items.q: What does the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies indicate?
items.correct: Space itself is expanding, stretching the wavelength of light from distant galaxies as it travels.
items.misconception: Galaxies are moving through space away from us, like objects flying outward from a centre.
items.random_wrong: The galaxies are turning red because they are getting older.
items.why: Students apply the Doppler effect literally; the expansion of space is a more subtle picture they often miss.
The answer examiners want is: 1 / H₀. “H₀” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Galaxy at 100 Mpc, H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc. v (km/s)?
items.why: Exact answer: 7000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
